title-group.article-title: Circulating Tumour DNA and Extracellular Vesicle‐Associated DNA as Biomarkers for Cancer Detection: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis

aff.named-content: Research Institute of the McGill University Health Centre

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Liquid biopsy is an emerging tool that allows the detection and monitoring of tumours through the analysis of various analytes in biofluids like blood. An important component of liquid biopsy is circulating tumour (ct) DNA, small fragments of DNA that are released by cancer cells, which have been shown to be a robust biomarker and are already in use clinically. Recent research indicates that cancer cells can also release DNA via extracellular vesicles (EVs), and this EV‐associated DNA (EV‐DNA) has emerged as another promising biomarker for several tumour types. However, its clinical utility remains under investigation. In this meta‐analysis, we compared the sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA for detecting cancer‐related mutations. Using the extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA database (EV‐ADD), which compiles all published literature on EV‐DNA, we systematically mined data comparing ctDNA and EV‐DNA in liquid biopsy as a cancer biomarker. After applying our inclusion criteria, we identified and analysed 12 studies involving 765 patients. Summary estimates showed a sensitivity of 64.6% (95% credible interval: 48.8%–79.4%) and specificity of 93.3% (95% credible interval: 86.8%–96.9%) for EV‐DNA, compared to a sensitivity of 70.0% (95% credible interval: 54.7%–83.4%) and a specificity of 90.6% (95% credible interval: 80.8%–96.0%) for ctDNA. Additionally, studies with higher EV‐ADD metric scores, reflecting adherence to MISEV guidelines, demonstrated higher probability of increased sensitivity and specificity for EV‐DNA and increased sensitivity for ctDNA. This analysis underscores the potential of EV‐DNA as a complementary biomarker to ctDNA in cancer detection. Future research should focus on larger patient cohorts and expand to other biofluids.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cancer remains a major clinical challenge despite significant advances in diagnostics and precision therapeutics. In the era of targeted treatments, molecular profiling of tumour tissue is crucial to personalise care. Although tissue biopsy has been traditionally used to determine a tumour's histologic grade and genetic alterations, its invasiveness limits the ability to perform real‐time, longitudinal sampling. Moreover, tissue biopsies can often fail to capture tumour heterogeneity because they provide only a spatially and temporally limited snapshot of a tumour (reviewed in Crowley et al. 2013). To address this need, liquid biopsy involves the analysis of bodily fluids, such as blood, urine, saliva, pleural effusion, and cerebrospinal fluid, to detect biomarkers less invasively. In the context of cancer, it allows longitudinal monitoring of tumour burden, treatment response and outcome. Many components from these bodily fluids can serve as liquid biopsy analytes, such as circulating tumour cells, extracellular vesicles (EVs), and cell‐free (cf) DNA and miRNAs (reviewed in Valencia and Montuenga 2021). EV‐associated components, such as RNA, proteins and more recently, DNA (EV‐DNA), have shown potential to be valuable cancer biomarkers (reviewed in Valencia and Montuenga 2021 and Tsering et al. 2024).

cfDNA consists of small DNA fragments that are released from cells mainly due to cell death, including apoptosis and necrosis, as well as through active secretion (Moss et al. 2018; Mattox et al. 2023). Circulating free DNA levels in blood serve as an independent prognostic marker as they correlate with tumour burden in various cancers like metastatic melanoma (Valpione et al. 2018), metastatic breast cancer (Cheng et al. 2018) and oral squamous cell carcinoma (Eboshida et al. 2024). Circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) is the fraction of cfDNA derived from tumours and can be recognised by characteristics from the originating cancer cells (reviewed in Siravegna et al. 2019 and Pessoa et al. 2020), like mutations (Husain et al. 2022; Zhang et al. 2020; Kirchweger et al. 2022; Wang et al. 2022; Tie et al. 2016; Liu et al. 2021; Tie et al. 2022; Moding et al. 2020), methylation (Stutheit‐Zhao et al. 2024; Grützmann et al. 2008; Fiano et al. 2014; Wong et al. 2003), fragmentation patterns (Underhill et al. 2016; Jiang et al. 2015; Ivanov et al. 2015; Liu 2022; Thierry 2023) and viral sequences (Ferrier et al. 2023; Chera et al. 2020; Leung et al. 2021; Chan et al. 2002; Yalçın et al. 2020). Another contributor to cfDNA is thought to be DNA associated with EVs (Cai et al. 2013; Lee et al. 2014; Kahlert et al. 2014). EVs are naturally occurring nanoparticles composed of a lipid bilayer membrane, ranging from 30 nm to 10 µm in diameter, are highly heterogenous and can be categorised based on their size, shape and origin (reviewed in Zaborowski et al. 2015; Spinelli et al. 2018; Cocucci and Meldolesi 2015; van Niel et al. 2018). A major physiological function of EVs is to transport cargo such as RNA (reviewed in Abels and Breakefield 2016; Jeppesen et al. 2019; Bellingham et al. 2012), DNA (Kahlert et al. 2014; Thakur et al. 2014; Lee et al. 2016) and proteins (Théry et al. 2001; Tauro et al. 2013; Liang et al. 2013) and act as messengers between cells, playing a vital role in intercellular communication (reviewed in Zaborowski et al. 2015; van Niel et al. 2018; Inamdar et al. 2017).

Although the field is still emerging, interest in EV‐DNA as a biomarker for cancer detection and monitoring has grown substantially in recent years (reviewed in Malkin and Bratman 2020). EV‐DNA and ctDNA are derived from different biological processes and may carry distinct molecular signatures. As ctDNA is predominantly released during apoptosis and necrosis (Choi et al. 2005), it reflects ongoing cell death and serves as a dynamic marker of tumour burden or treatment response (reviewed in Stejskal et al. 2023). In contrast, EV‐DNA is actively secreted by viable cells, often packaged within EVs (Takahashi et al. 2017) and may capture signals from functionally active tumour cells and the surrounding tumour microenvironment (Lee et al. 2014; Chennakrishnaiah et al. 2020). Unlike the typically short and fragmented nature of ctDNA, EV‐DNA can include longer and more intact genomic regions (Kahlert et al. 2014), potentially offering greater resolution for mutational or epigenetic analysis. These mechanistic differences suggest that EV‐DNA may provide complementary insights into tumour biology and enhance diagnostic sensitivity, particularly in early‐stage disease or treatment‐responsive settings. Understanding these distinctions is critical to evaluate how EV‐DNA and ctDNA can be used most effectively—either individually or in combination—in liquid biopsy applications for cancer detection.

Currently, there are six liquid biopsy tests approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) that employ ctDNA as a biomarker for cancer: CancerSEEK, Guardant360 CDx, FoundationOne Liquid CDx, Cologuard and EpiColon (reviewed in Choi and Kim 2022). Although the FDA has approved liquid biopsy tests that detect cancer mutations using ctDNA, the same is not true for EV‐DNA. This is partly due to the recency of EV‐associated DNA's discovery, which was only reported for the first time 11 years ago (Cai et al. 2013). An advantage of EV‐DNA compared to ctDNA is its ability to more comprehensively recapitulate the genome and mutational burden of the parental tumour due to protection by the lipid bilayer, while the latter is fragmented and thus more difficult to obtain the specific DNA mutations pursued in a given analysis (reviewed in Valencia and Montuenga 2021). However, ctDNA is easier to obtain from biofluids, as EV‐DNA still faces challenges related to isolation, purity, yield, downstream analyses and reproducibility (reviewed in Tsering et al. 2024). Hence, it would be useful to compare EV‐DNA and ctDNA as liquid biopsy‐based cancer biomarkers.

Despite the promising role of EV‐DNA, technical challenges persist. Separating EVs from non‐vesicular extracellular particles (NVEPs) is technically challenging, and standard EV isolation protocols, such as centrifugation or commercial kits, do not fully separate EVs and NVEPs (Lötvall et al. 2014). Moreover, depending on the protocol, the composition of isolated EVs, including proteins and nucleic acids, can differ greatly (Lötvall et al. 2014). Along with this challenge, the large heterogeneity of EVs makes the development of biomarkers difficult since appropriate and accurate methods are needed for each step of EV isolation and analysis. Hence, the Minimal Information for Studies of Extracellular Vesicles (MISEVs) was created in 2014 (Lötvall et al. 2014), updated in 2018 (Thery et al. 2018), and its third version, MISEV2023, was published in early 2024 (Welsh et al. 2024). The MISEV provides guidelines that promote rigour and standardisation across various aspects of EV work, including EV isolation, characterisation, and analysis.

Many databases have been developed to standardise research on EV‐associated proteins, RNA, lipids and metabolites. These include ExoCarta (Keerthikumar et al. 2016), Vesiclepedia (Kalra et al. 2012, Pathan et al. 2018), EVpedia (Kim et al. 2013), and EV‐TRACK (Van Deun et al. 2017). To fill the gap in such resources for EV‐DNA research, we previously manually curated a database of EV‐DNA data derived from human biofluids (liquid biopsy) and in vitro studies called the extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA database (EV‐ADD) (Tsering et al. 2022). The EV‐ADD reports experimental data extracted from peer‐reviewed publications on EV‐DNA, including type of disease, number of patients enrolled in the study, type of biofluid, volume of biofluids, EV isolation method, EV characterisation, EV‐DNA isolation technique, EV‐DNA type, EV‐DNA detection method, EV‐DNA fragment size, targeted gene(s), enzymatic treatment, results, and applications of EV‐DNA (Tsering et al. 2022). Moreover, an EV‐ADD data metric system was also integrated to assign a compliance score to the MISEV guidelines based on a variety of experimental parameters reported in each study.

Although the potential of EV‐DNA as a cancer biomarker has been demonstrated in many tumour types (Möhrmann et al. 2018; Nakai et al. 2022; Choi et al. 2022; Thakur et al. 2021; Wan et al. 2018; Glennon et al. 2021; Bernard et al. 2019), its clinical utility, particularly compared to ctDNA, remains under investigation (Tkach et al. 2022). Previous meta‐analyses have shown that EVs (Liu et al. 2021) and EV‐associated RNA and proteins (Jia et al. 2022; Li et al. 2023; Lee et al. 2022) are promising biomarkers for cancer diagnosis. However, no meta‐analyses have been conducted on the clinical utility of EV‐DNA in detecting mutations as a cancer biomarker. Thus, this study conducted a meta‐analysis, including 12 studies and 765 participants, to investigate the potential clinical utility of EV‐DNA in liquid biopsy for cancer detection by comparing the sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA reported by studies from EV‐ADD. This meta‐analysis has important implications for cohorts that are afflicted with cancers in which EV‐DNA may be a reliable and appropriate biomarker and should be investigated.

sec.title: Search Strategy and Study Selection
sec.p: This meta‐analysis was conducted per the guidelines in the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA) and prospectively registered with PROSPERO (CRD42024552523). An electronic search was performed using the EV‐ADD. All studies were included until 31 January 2025. The full texts of selected studies were reviewed to determine eligibility. The inclusion criteria for the analysis were: (1) clinical studies including individuals diagnosed with any cancer; (2) studies that compared the sensitivity and specificity of detecting cancer‐specific mutations in EV‐DNA and ctDNA in human biofluids against tissue biopsies (reference standard); (3) studies that reported the concordance between tumour and EV‐DNA or between tumour and ctDNA; (4) in cases of multiple detection methods, the most commonly used method was selected relative to the rest of the studies. Studies that were unrelated to cancer, related to cancer but did not compare EV‐DNA to ctDNA, investigated mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), and compared the concordance between EV‐DNA and ctDNA, but not tumour, were excluded.

sec.title: Data Extraction
sec.p: The following data were extracted from the included articles: title, sample size (including true positives, false negatives, true negatives and false positives), cancer subtype, target mutation(s), method(s) of EV‐DNA/ctDNA analysis, sensitivity of EV‐DNA/ctDNA, specificity of EV‐DNA/ctDNA, EV‐DNA and ctDNA concentrations, EV‐ADD score, and main conclusion of the study.

sec.title: Quality Assessment
sec.p: The Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies 2 (QUADAS‐2) was used to assess the quality of the included studies. Four domains (patient selection, index test, reference standard, and flow and timing) are evaluated regarding the risk of bias. The first three domains (patient selection, index test, and reference standard) are also assessed in terms of concerns about applicability. Signalling questions are included to assist in assessing the risk of bias. These questions are answered as ‘yes’, ‘no’, or ‘unclear’ and are framed such that ‘yes’ suggests a low risk of bias. A study's domain has a low risk of bias if the answers to all signalling questions about this domain are ‘yes’. There is potential for bias if any signalling question is answered ‘no’. In these cases, we judged a domain's risk of bias based on the relative importance of the signalling question. The concerns about applicability are rated as ‘low’, ‘high’, or ‘unclear’ and are assessed based on information from each study.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Wilson score 95% confidence intervals were computed for both the sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA for each study. The Wilson score method is particularly appropriate when dealing with small sample sizes or extreme proportions, as it provides better coverage probability compared to other methods like the Wald interval.

A Bayesian bivariate model was employed to calculate sensitivity and specificity summary estimates for both EV‐DNA and ctDNA, along with 95% credible intervals (Sutton and Abrams 2001). This approach is superior to simple proportional meta‐analysis for diagnostic test accuracy, as it accounts for the correlation between sensitivity and specificity and allows for between‐study heterogeneity. The contribution of each study to the summary estimates was based on the inverse of their variance. This method gives more weight to studies with more precise estimates (lower variance), which is a standard approach in meta‐analysis to ensure that more reliable studies significantly influence the overall results. The Bayesian approach allows for direct probability statements about the parameters of interest, providing a more intuitive interpretation than traditional frequentist p values. Statistical significance of the difference between two sensitivities or specificities was assessed by examining whether the posterior probability of the difference exceeding zero was very low (e.g., less than 0.05) or very high (e.g., greater than 0.95).

The EV‐ADD score, measuring compliance with international guidelines on EV research, was incorporated into the analysis through Bayesian meta‐regression. The dichotomisation of the EV‐ADD score into ‘low’ and ‘high’ categories based on the median value was done to facilitate subgroup comparisons and to enhance the interpretability of the results. It allows for comparison between studies with different levels of guideline compliance, potentially revealing the impact of methodological quality on diagnostic accuracy. The EV‐ADD score was also treated as a continuous variable and plotted against EV‐DNA sensitivity/specificity and ctDNA sensitivity/specificity.

The Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm was implemented with careful consideration of convergence and autocorrelation. Three independent chains were used to assess convergence. A burn‐in period of 15,000 iterations was discarded. A total of 100,000 iterations were kept for each chain, resulting in 300,000 total iterations. Thinning was applied by keeping every 10th iteration to reduce autocorrelation. The final posterior sample consisted of 30,000 iterations.

An unpaired two‐tailed Student's t test was used to compare the means of EV‐DNA/ctDNA sensitivity between two independent groups (early‐ and late‐stage patients). Results were considered statistically significant at a p value less than 0.05. Analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel. Data are presented as median with individual values.

sec.title: Eligible Studies
sec.p: As of 31 January 2025, a total of 227 studies were included in the EV‐ADD database. Of these, 108 were excluded for being either purely in vitro studies (n = 103) or animal studies (n = 5), leaving 119 studies for full‐text review. Following full‐text screening, 91 studies were excluded because they were either unrelated to cancer, focused on cancer but not on liquid biopsy, did not report sensitivity and specificity data for both EV‐DNA and ctDNA in detecting cancer mutations, or did not report concordance between tumour biopsy and EV‐DNA or tumour biopsy and ctDNA (Figure 1). Of the remaining 28 studies, 21 analysed plasma samples from patient blood, while 7 focused on other biofluids, including pleural effusion (n = 2), bone marrow (n = 2), urine (n = 1) and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (n = 2). Studies using non‐plasma biofluids were excluded, as cross‐biofluid comparisons were deemed inappropriate and subgroup analysis was not feasible due to the small number of studies per biofluid, which would have resulted in unreliable estimates of between‐study variability. Thus, we proceeded with studies that performed liquid biopsy from plasma samples (n = 21). Of these, two assessed concordance between EV‐DNA and tumour tissue and between ctDNA and tumour tissue without reporting sensitivity and specificity and were thus excluded. Two studies investigated EV‐associated and cf mtDNA and were excluded due to lack of comparability to genomic DNA‐focused studies. Five studies detected oncogene mutations without confirming their presence in the patient's tumour tissue and were also thus excluded. This resulted in a total of 12 studies included in the final meta‐analysis (Figure 1).

caption.p: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA) flow diagram of literature search and study selection. BALF, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid; BM, bone marrow; EV‐ADD, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA database; mtDNA, mitochondrial DNA; PE, pleural effusion; sens, sensitivity; spec, specificity.

sec.title: Study Characteristics
sec.p: A total of 12 studies with 765 patients were included in the analysis (Figure 1). There was variation in the number of patients included per study, ranging from 4 to 446 (Table 1). Of the 12 studies included, 8 (67%) were prospective and 4 (33%) were retrospective. The most common cancer types among the studies were colorectal cancer and breast cancer with three studies (25%) investigating each cancer type. Other cancers that were examined included non‐small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, ovarian cancer, papillary thyroid cancer, appendiceal cancer, endometrial cancer, Erdheim–Chester disease, melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, and oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. EDTA tubes were the most used blood collection tube, being used in 10/12 studies (83%). One study (8%) used citrate tubes, and 1 study (8%) did not mention the blood collection tube used. For both ctDNA and EV‐DNA, droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) was the most common method [used in 10 studies (83%) to detect ctDNA mutations and 9 studies (75%) to detect EV‐DNA mutations]. Other methods included next‐generation sequencing and an amplification‐refractory mutation system (Figure 2).

caption.p: Summary of study and patient characteristics.

caption.p: Visual summary of study characteristics. The study design, cancer subtype, blood tube used, ctDNA extraction method, EV‐DNA extraction method, ctDNA detection method and EV‐DNA detection method used in each of the 12 included studies are shown in distinct colours. ctDNA, circulating tumour DNA; EV‐DNA, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA.

sec.title: EV‐DNA and ctDNA Have High Sensitivity and Specificity in Detecting Mutations in Liquid Biopsy
sec.p: The sensitivity of EV‐DNA in detecting mutations was reported in 6 studies (50%) and deduced in 6 studies (50%), while the specificity was reported in 5 studies (42%), deduced in 4 studies (33%) and not computable in 3 studies (25%). For ctDNA, 6 studies (50%) reported, and 6 studies (50%) allowed us to deduce sensitivity. The specificity of ctDNA was reported in 5 studies (42%), deduced in 3 studies (25%) and not computable in 4 studies (33%). The sensitivity and specificity for EV‐DNA extracted from plasma were 64.6% (95% credible interval: 48.8%, 79.4%) and 93.3% (95% credible interval: 86.8%, 96.9%), respectively (Figure 3). The sensitivity and specificity for ctDNA extracted from plasma were 70.0% (95% credible interval: 54.7%, 83.4%) and 90.6% (95% credible interval: 80.8%, 96.0%), respectively (Figure 4). ctDNA has an increased probability of higher sensitivity, while EV‐DNA has an increased probability of higher specificity (Table S1).

caption.p: Sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA from each included study and the summary estimates. Forest plot of pooled meta‐analysis including 12 studies. CI, confidence interval; EV‐DNA, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA; FN, false negative; FP, false positive; Sens, sensitivity; Spec, specificity; TN, true negative; TP, true positive.
Sensitivity and specificity of circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) from each included study and the summary estimates. Forest plot of pooled meta‐analysis including 12 studies.

sec.title: Complying With Guidelines on EV Research Is Associated With Increased Sensitivity and Specificity for EV‐DNA and Increased Sensitivity for ctDNA
sec.p: We aimed next to determine whether studies that better complied with international guidelines on EV research yielded higher sensitivity and specificity for EV‐DNA. The MISEV guidelines promote rigour and standardisation across all aspects of EV work, including EV isolation, characterisation, and analysis. In the EV‐ADD, we previously designed a metric system that assigns a score to each study based on its compliance with MISEV guidelines. Specifically, the score is based on the reporting of sample preparation, EV isolation, and characterisation (Tsering et al. 2022). A study with a high EV‐ADD score adheres highly to MISEV guidelines. A study with a low EV‐ADD score lacks reporting in the previously mentioned aspects, potentially raising concerns about EV isolation yield, quality, and downstream analysis of EV‐DNA.

The median EV‐ADD score for EV‐DNA studies was 0.57. In our subgroup analysis, we computed the sensitivity and specificity summary estimates using only studies with low EV‐ADD scores (≤0.57) or only studies with high EV‐ADD scores (>0.57). The EV‐DNA detection rates in patients from studies with a high EV‐ADD score were pooled, resulting in a sensitivity of 66.7% (95% credible interval: 43.1%, 86.1%) and a specificity of 94.2% (95% credible interval: 85.7%, 97.8%) (Figure 5). The EV‐DNA detection rates in patients from studies with a low EV‐ADD score were also pooled, resulting in a sensitivity of 62.7% (95% credible interval: 37.0%, 83.3%) and a specificity of 92.1% (95% credible interval: 78.5%, 97.8%) (Figure 5). Although the summary estimates suggest modestly better diagnostic accuracy in studies with higher EV‐ADD scores, the large credible and prediction intervals and small number of studies in each group reflect a considerable amount of uncertainty (Table S2).

caption.p: The sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA were measured from studies with a high EV‐ADD score (top) and studies with a low EV‐ADD score (bottom), along with the summary estimates. Forest plot of meta‐regression analyses including 12 studies. EV‐ADD, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA database; EV‐DNA, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA.

sec.p: We then determined whether we would observe a similar trend with ctDNA. The median EV‐ADD score for ctDNA studies was 0.57. The ctDNA sensitivity and specificity for studies with a high EV‐ADD score were 77.9% (95% credible interval: 58.7%, 91.3%) and 86.4% (95% credible interval: 68.2%, 95.1%), respectively (Figure 6). The ctDNA sensitivity and specificity for studies with a low EV‐ADD score were 60.5% (95% credible interval: 37.2%, 81.3%) and 96.8% (95% credible interval: 87.8%, 99.6%), respectively (Figure 6). Similar to EV‐DNA, the large credible and prediction intervals and small number of studies in each group suggest a significant amount of uncertainty, even if the summary estimates suggest higher sensitivity in studies with higher EV‐ADD scores and higher specificity in studies with lower EV‐ADD scores (Table S3). To further determine the relationship between EV‐ADD score and sensitivity/specificity, we also treated the EV‐ADD score as a continuous variable and plotted it against EV‐DNA sensitivity, EV‐DNA specificity, ctDNA sensitivity and ctDNA specificity, respectively. A linear regression model was applied to assess the correlation between the dependent and independent variables. Surprisingly, no correlation was found between EV‐ADD score and EV‐DNA sensitivity (R 2 = 0.0657), EV‐ADD score and EV‐DNA specificity (R 2 = 0.0137), EV‐ADD score and ctDNA sensitivity (R 2 = 0.1263) and EV‐ADD score and ctDNA specificity (R 2 = 0.2405) (Figure S1).

caption.p: The sensitivity and specificity of ctDNA were measured from studies with a high EV‐ADD score (top) and studies with a low EV‐ADD score (bottom), along with the summary estimates. Forest plot of meta‐regression analyses including 12 studies. ctDNA, circulating tumour DNA; EV‐ADD, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA database.

sec.title: Sensitivity in Detecting Cancer Mutations Is Significantly Higher in Late‐Stage Patients Compared to Early‐Stage Patients
sec.p: The ability of liquid biopsy to detect cancer‐related mutations in early‐ and late‐stage patients has important implications for diagnosis, treatment response and monitoring minimal residual disease. Several studies have shown that ctDNA is found more frequently in later‐stage disease (Bettegowda et al. 2014; Yang et al. 2018). Here, we performed a subgroup analysis to determine the sensitivity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA in detecting mutations from early‐ (Stages I and II) and late‐stage (Stages III and IV) patients. Five studies included in our meta‐analysis included both early‐ and late‐stage patients (Choi et al. 2022; Thakur et al. 2021; Wan et al. 2018; Glennon et al. 2021; Bernard et al. 2019). The mean sensitivity of EV‐DNA in early‐ and late‐stage patients was 0.48 ± 0.36 and 0.87 ± 0.12, respectively (p = 0.052). The mean sensitivity of ctDNA in early‐ and late‐stage patients was 0.36 ± 0.22 and 0.85 ± 0.10, respectively (p = 0.001) (Figure 7). In early‐stage disease, EV‐DNA had higher sensitivity than ctDNA (0.48 vs. 0.36)—although this difference was not significant. These findings support further investigation of EV‐DNA as a complementary approach to ctDNA, particularly for enhancing mutation detection in early‐stage cancer.

caption.p: Subgroup analysis of (a) EV‐DNA and (b) ctDNA sensitivity in early‐ versus late‐stage disease. Panel C: Sensitivity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA in early‐stage patients. Panel D: Sensitivity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA in late‐stage patients. Panel E: Individual sensitivities of EV‐DNA and ctDNA from the included studies along with the mean and standard deviation. ctDNA, circulating tumour DNA; EV‐DNA, extracellular vesicle‐associated DNA.

sec.p: For the QUADAS‐2 quality assessment (Figure 8), 9 studies (75%) had a low risk of bias, 1 study had a high risk of bias (8%), and 2 studies (16%) had an unclear risk of bias for flow and timing, 12 studies (100%) had a low risk of bias for the reference standard, 12 studies (100%) had a high risk of bias for the index tests, and 12 studies (100%) had a low risk of bias for patient selection. Twelve studies (100%) had low concerns regarding applicability for the reference standard, 12 studies (100%) had low concerns regarding applicability for the index tests, and 12 studies (100%) had low concerns regarding applicability for patient selection.

caption.p: Graphical representation of the summarised Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies 2 (QUADAS‐2) results regarding (a) risk of bias and (b) applicability.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This meta‐analysis sought to compare ctDNA and EV‐DNA as a cancer biomarker. Overall, we found that EV‐DNA and ctDNA were comparable in detecting cancer‐related mutations, with no significant differences in sensitivity and specificity. Subgroup analysis by EV‐ADD score, a measure of compliance to MISEV guidelines, showed that studies with higher EV‐ADD scores have an increased probability of higher EV‐DNA sensitivity, EV‐DNA specificity, and ctDNA sensitivity. Altogether the data in our analysis point to the potential of EV‐DNA as a biomarker and highlight the need for stringent adherence to guidelines for EV isolation and analysis.

To our knowledge, this is the first meta‐analysis comparing the detection of cancer mutations in EV‐DNA relative to ctDNA in multiple cancers. In the literature, meta‐analyses have been mainly conducted to assess the sensitivity and specificity of ctDNA as a cancer biomarker in individual or groups of related cancers, such as breast cancer (Galvano et al. 2022), colorectal cancer (Tang et al. 2017; Hao et al. 2017; Min et al. 2023; Ye et al. 2021), esophageal cancer (Chidambaram and Markar 2022), gastric cancer (Gao et al. 2017), upper gastrointestinal cancers (Creemers et al. 2017), NSCLC (Passiglia et al. 2018; Qiu et al. 2015; Zhou et al. 2020; Chen et al. 2024), pancreatic cancer (Zhu et al. 2020), and hepatocellular carcinoma (Zhang et al. 2020). The pooled sensitivities of ctDNA analysis for these meta‐analyses vary widely from 28% (Creemers et al. 2017) to 77% (Ye et al. 2021), while the pooled specificities range from 80% (Passiglia et al. 2018) to 99% (Chidambaram and Markar 2022; Chen et al. 2024). In our study, the summary estimates of sensitivity we obtained for EV‐DNA and ctDNA were 64.6% (95% credible interval: 48.8%, 79.4%) and 70.0% (95% credible interval: 54.7%, 83.4%), respectively. Although the sensitivity values are in a similar range to other meta‐analyses mentioned above, the 95% credible interval of our meta‐analysis is much larger, indicating the possible presence of heterogeneity that is likely due to the inclusion of studies investigating various cancer types. Indeed, the shedding rates of ctDNA (Bettegowda et al. 2014) and EV‐DNA (Möhrmann et al. 2018) have been previously described to vary between cancer types, with a smaller variance in EV‐DNA (Möhrmann et al. 2018). The summary estimates of specificity we obtained for EV‐DNA and ctDNA were 93.3% (95% credible interval: 86.8%, 96.9%) and 90.6% (95% credible interval: 80.8%, 96.0%), respectively. In this case, the specificity values and 95% credible interval are more similar to other meta‐analyses, suggesting that the fewer studies included in the meta‐analysis for specificity calculations were more homogeneous compared to the studies included in the meta‐analysis for sensitivity calculations.

Although EV‐DNA generally had higher Bayesian probabilities of increased sensitivity and specificity than ctDNA, it is context‐dependent. For instance, in renal cell carcinoma (Glennon et al. 2021), ctDNA had higher sensitivity than EV‐DNA (45% vs. 36%). In oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (Nguyen et al. 2020), ctDNA had a sensitivity of 91% while EV‐DNA had a sensitivity of 42%. Therefore, the choice of EV‐DNA or ctDNA for liquid biopsy should take the specific disease into account. Breast cancer, oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, NSCLC, colorectal cancer, and multiple myeloma have been shown to shed more cfDNA than EV‐DNA, which could indicate that ctDNA is a better choice to detect mutations in these diseases (Nakai et al. 2022; Thakur et al. 2021; Wan et al. 2018; Tkach et al. 2022; Nguyen et al. 2020; Heestermans et al. 2022). Sensitivity also varied between studies investigating cohorts afflicted with the same cancer. For example, the sensitivity of detecting mutations from EV‐DNA in breast cancer patients ranged from 23% (Nakai et al. 2022) to 67% (Cardinali et al. 2022) depending on the study, while the ctDNA sensitivity ranged from 31% (Nakai et al. 2022) to 100% (Cardinali et al. 2022) in those same studies. In colon cancer, EV‐DNA sensitivity ranged from 62% (Thakur et al. 2021) to 100% (Galbiati et al. 2021) while ctDNA sensitivity ranged from 67% (Thakur et al. 2021) to 100% (Galbiati et al. 2021).

Wan et al. (2018) found no correlation between the levels of EV‐DNA and cfDNA in NSCLC patients, suggesting the release mechanisms and perhaps the origin of these two sources of DNA are different. On the other hand, hepatocellular carcinoma tumours have been shown to shed more EV‐associated mtDNA copy numbers than cf mtDNA copy numbers (Li et al. 2020). Reports have also indicated that tumours in lung adenocarcinoma and melanoma and aggressive mastocytosis emit more EV‐DNA than cf/ctDNA (Lee et al. 2018; Klump et al. 2018). By comparing levels between studies, EV‐DNA levels were found to be higher in NSCLC (Wan et al. 2018) compared to breast (Nakai et al. 2022) and colorectal (Thakur et al. 2021) cancers. The detection of EGFR mutations had an overall sensitivity of 46% and specificity of 97% for EV‐DNA and an overall sensitivity of 40% and specificity of 92% for ctDNA (Wan et al. 2018). However, the sensitivity of both EV‐DNA and ctDNA in detecting EGFR mutations was relatively low in patients with early‐stage NSCLC—26% for EV‐DNA and 14% for ctDNA. In contrast, detection rates for EGFR T790M were much higher in late‐stage NSCLC—79% for EV‐DNA and 82% for ctDNA (Wan et al. 2018). Similarly, in the context of colon cancer, there was also a trend towards increased detection in late‐stage patients (Thakur et al. 2021). Taken together, the data suggest higher sensitivity for both EV‐DNA and ctDNA in later‐stage patients; however, our subgroup analysis revealed that the difference was only statistically significant for ctDNA. This likely arises from the challenge of accurately distinguishing cfDNA sources, as the marked rise in cfDNA during advanced cancer complicates source attribution. Prioritising early‐stage disease will enable clearer delineation of ctDNA and EV‐DNA subtypes, improving mechanistic insight and biomarker accuracy. These results highlight the influence of disease stage on the performance of liquid biopsy assays.

The source of EVs for liquid biopsy assays may impact the sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA and ctDNA. Indeed, studies have demonstrated higher sensitivity and specificity of ctDNA and EV‐DNA from BALF than from plasma in NSCLC (Hur et al. 2018; Kim et al. 2022). Ascites‐derived EV‐DNA was also shown to better represent tumour copy number variation status than plasma‐derived EV‐DNA in ovarian cancer (Yokoi et al. 2019). In bladder cancer, urine‐derived EV‐DNA was found to be a better biomarker than cfDNA or serum‐derived EV‐DNA (Zhou et al. 2021). Finally, in glioma, ctDNA derived from cerebrospinal fluid was found to carry more tumour‐specific mutations than plasma‐derived ctDNA (Maire et al. 2021). These findings suggest that choosing the appropriate biofluid to analyse EV‐DNA and ctDNA is crucial and context‐dependent.

The results of the meta‐regression with a dichotomised EV‐ADD score supported our hypothesis that studies that were more MISEV‐compliant have higher probabilities of increased sensitivity and specificity (Tables S2 and S3). This finding aligns with our expectation, as the more stringent EV isolation methods yield purer EV samples, which are likely to enhance assay performance. The same association was not seen between EV‐ADD score and ctDNA specificity (Table S3). A study's EV‐ADD score is based on its reporting of biofluid processing, EV purification, EV‐DNA isolation and EV characterisation (Tsering et al. 2022). A high EV‐ADD score, which corresponds to high adherence to standardised MISEV guidelines and protocols, can help ensure that a specific EV subpopulation and its corresponding cargo were isolated. Interestingly, when we plotted the EV‐ADD score against the sensitivity and specificity of EV‐DNA or ctDNA, there was no correlation in either liquid biopsy analyte. This may be due to several reasons. First, perhaps only very low compliance (e.g., EV‐ADD score <0.1) or very high compliance (e.g., EV‐ADD score >0.9) affects sensitivity and specificity, which would be missed by a linear regression model. Second, the EV‐ADD score aggregates many factors (e.g., pre‐analytical steps, characterisation, and quantification), whereas potentially only a subset (e.g., purity checks) truly affects sensitivity and specificity. Large EVs have been shown to carry the majority of tumour DNA circulating in the plasma of prostate cancer patients (Vagner et al. 2018). Encapsulation within the lipid bilayer may protect this DNA from degradation by circulating DNases, potentially enhancing its stability as a biomarker (Cai et al. 2013; Kahlert et al. 2014; Vagner et al. 2018; Degli Esposti et al. 2021). This increased stability could improve sensitivity, as EV‐associated DNA would be more readily preserved and accessible for downstream analysis. The limitation of EV‐DNA as a biomarker is that it is less ‘real‐time’ since it may not reflect the disease at that moment, compared to ctDNA, whose half‐life ranges from a few minutes (Rumore et al. 1992; Lo et al. 1999) to hours (Lau et al. 2002; Yu et al. 2013). However, EV‐DNA's lack of real‐time monitoring could be complemented by ctDNA, highlighting the utility of using both analytes in combination.

It is also important to note that the method of EV isolation can influence downstream DNA extraction as methods vary in how effectively they separate EVs from cfDNA. These differences may therefore bias comparisons between ctDNA and EV‐DNA. For example, size‐exclusion chromatography (SEC) may not efficiently separate cfDNA from EVs, whereas density‐gradient ultracentrifugation (DGUC) may remove external EV‐DNA altogether (Liu et al. 2022). Thus, if EV‐DNA extraction via SEC includes external cfDNA, the EV‐DNA signal may be artificially inflated, making EV‐DNA appear more sensitive than it truly is. Conversely, DGUC removes external EV‐DNA, so EV‐DNA measurements reflect only internal cargo. This could make EV‐DNA seem less sensitive compared to ctDNA, even if EVs carry relevant tumour signals.

Although this analysis has focused on mutation detection in EV‐DNA as a diagnostic biomarker, it is also important to mention the role of EV‐DNA as a functional biomarker. Recent studies have demonstrated that EV‐DNA can interact with proteins and signalling pathways, influencing tumour progression and metastasis, as shown in leukaemia (Cai et al. 2013; Cai et al. 2014) and liver cancer models (Wortzel et al. 2024). This functional dimension positions EV‐DNA not only as a diagnostic marker but also as a potential mechanistic biomarker that could inform therapeutic strategies. For example, colorectal cancer EVs have been shown to transfer EV‐DNA to recipient fibroblasts and possibly transcribe it in vitro (Abdouh et al. 2019). This transfer of DNA, along with miRNAs, may be responsible for the transformation of these fibroblasts into colon carcinoma cells. Moreover, EGFRvIII‐driven glioblastoma‐derived EVs carrying genomic DNA have been shown to be taken up by primary endothelial cells and induce the formation of micronuclei, an indicator of genetic instability, in the recipient cells (Chennakrishnaiah et al. 2020). Such works demonstrate that—unlike ctDNA, which primarily reflects tumour burden—EV‐DNA may provide insights into active disease processes, including metastatic spread.

A limitation of our meta‐analysis is that we excluded studies investigating mtDNA. Previous literature has shown that large‐scale mtDNA deletions detected from patient blood (reviewed in Mohd Khair et al. 2021) and circulating cf mtDNA (Hashad et al. 2015) have the potential to be mtDNA‐based cancer biomarkers. However, little is known about the utility of EV‐derived mtDNA as a biomarker. EV‐derived mtDNA from controls, hepatocellular carcinoma patients, and hepatitis patients has been shown to recapitulate the entire mitochondrial genome (Li et al. 2020). In the same study, they also reported that EV‐derived mtDNA fragment length and copy number varied between controls and hepatocellular carcinoma patients (Li et al. 2020). These findings highlight the need for further studies to clarify the diagnostic and prognostic utility of EV‐derived mtDNA.

Several important studies in the field of EV‐DNA liquid biopsy did not meet our inclusion criteria for meta‐analysis, but nevertheless provide valuable insights. For instance, Casanova‐Salas et al. (2024) reported a 55% detection rate for mutations in plasma EV‐DNA from metastatic prostate cancer patients, while Yang et al. (2017) detected KRAS G12D mutations in 40% of serum EV‐DNA samples from pancreatic cancer patients. Although methodological differences prevented their inclusion, these findings underscore the growing evidence base supporting EV‐DNA as a promising biomarker in oncology.

Although ctDNA assays showed higher sensitivity compared to EV‐DNA (70.0% vs. 64.6%) and EV‐DNA assays demonstrated higher specificity compared to ctDNA (93.3% vs. 90.6%), the large credible and prediction intervals and the small number of studies in each group reflect a considerable amount of uncertainty. Moreover, ctDNA is already integrated into several FDA‐approved liquid biopsy tests, underscoring its established clinical utility. Nonetheless, EV‐DNA represents a promising complementary approach: it can be isolated using existing workflows with minimal procedural changes and may offer additional biological insights. To advance EV‐DNA towards clinical adoption, we recommend the development of standardised protocols, prospective validation studies—particularly in high‐risk cohorts—to assess its potential value alongside approved ctDNA tests.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This meta‐analysis is the first to determine the summary estimates of sensitivity and specificity for detecting mutations in both EV‐DNA and ctDNA. We highlight the exciting potential of EV‐DNA as a complementary cancer biomarker. The diversity in study populations and publication bias clearly demands additional research to firmly establish these findings and enhance the effectiveness of EV‐DNA and ctDNA analysis in the treatment of various cancers. Tackling these limitations with more robust study designs, consistent methodologies, thorough data reporting, and meticulous attention to potential biases is essential for propelling our understanding of the prognostic significance of EV‐DNA and ctDNA mutations in various cancers.
